q: A loop <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of straight edges has six corner points <math><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>C</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>D</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>E</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mi>F</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> . The magnetic field in this region is <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi>T</mi></math> . The quantity of flux through the loop <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> (in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> ) is _____________

The magnetic field in this region is <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi>T</mi></math> . The quantity of flux through the loop <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> (in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> ) is _____________
solution:
ϕ=B→.A→=3i^+4k^.25i^+25k^

ϕ=2×25+4×25=175weber

subject: Physics
q: Two coils have mutual inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>002</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. The current changes in the first coil according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mi>π</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>emf</mi></math> in the second coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is
question: Two coils have mutual inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>002</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. The current changes in the first coil according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mi>π</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>emf</mi></math> in the second coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is
solution:
The amount of flux induced in the second coil due to the first one is given by

ϕ2=Mi=Mi0sinωt   ...1

where, M is the mutual inductance of the coils.

The formula to calculate the emf induced can be written as

ε=-dϕ2dt   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that,

εmax=Mi0ω=0.002×5×50π V=π2 V

Hence, α=2.

q: A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively moves with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> axis direction, in a space containing a variable magnetic field in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. The field has a gradient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction and it is decreasing with time at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>Ω</mi></math>, the power dissipated by the loop as heat is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively moves with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> axis direction, in a space containing a variable magnetic field in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. The field has a gradient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction and it is decreasing with time at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>Ω</mi></math>, the power dissipated by the loop as heat is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Let B0 is the magnetic field at origin. Then,

dBdx=-10-310-2

⇒∫B0BdB=-∫0x10-1dx

⇒B-B0=-10-1x

⇒B=B0-x10

Motional emf in AB=0

Motional emf in CD=0

Motional emf in AD=ε1=B0ℓv

Magnetic field on rod BC 

=B0--12×10-210

Motional emf in  BC=ε2=B0+12×10-210ℓ×v

Net equivalent emf εeq=ε2-ε1=300×10-7 V

For time variation

εeq'=AdBdt=60×10-7 V

εeqnet =εeq+εeq'=360×10-7 V

Power r=εeq net 2R=216×10-9 W

solution:

$C=\frac{1}{4 \times \pi^2 f^2 \times 10}$

subject: Physics
q: A coil is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. When the field is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, an induced emf of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is produced in the coil. If the diameter of the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, then the number of turns in the coil is:
question: A coil is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. When the field is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, an induced emf of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is produced in the coil. If the diameter of the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, then the number of turns in the coil is:
solution:
Average induced EMF is given by, ε=N∆ϕt

Also, change in flux ∆ϕ=(∆B)A

Given: Bi=5000 T,

Bf=3000 T

d=0.02 m ⇒r=d2=0.01 m

Hence, 

∆ϕ=(∆B)A

=2000π0.012=0.2π

EMF, ε=N∆ϕt⇒22=N0.2π2

⇒N=70

subject: Physics
q: A conducting circular loop is placed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane in presence of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> in SI unit. If the radius of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the induced emf in the loop, at time, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A conducting circular loop is placed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane in presence of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> in SI unit. If the radius of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the induced emf in the loop, at time, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
By the faraday's law in electromagnetic induction

εind=-dϕdt=-dB→·A→dt

Magnetic flux is ϕ=B→·A→=3t3j^+3t2k^·π12k^

So, ϕ=3t2π

Now, induced emf is εind=dϕdt=6tπ

At t=2, εind=62π=12π V.

Thus, the value of n=12.

subject: Physics
q: A coil of self inductance <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and resistance of <math><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected through a switch to a battery of internal resistance <math><mtext> </mtext><mn>0.9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> . After the switch is closed, the time taken for the current to attain <math><mn>80</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> of the saturation value is: <math> <mrow> <mrow><mo>[</mo> <mrow> <mi>ln</mi><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn></mrow> <mo>]</mo></mrow></mrow> </math>
question: A coil of self inductance <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and resistance of <math><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected through a switch to a battery of internal resistance <math><mtext> </mtext><mn>0.9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> . After the switch is closed, the time taken for the current to attain <math><mn>80</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> of the saturation value is: <math> <mrow> <mrow><mo>[</mo> <mrow> <mi>ln</mi><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn></mrow> <mo>]</mo></mrow></mrow> </math>
solution:
The instantaneous current in an LR circuit is given by the equation :
(instantaneous current) I=Is1-e-RtL

Where I, Is, R, t, L are the instantaneous current, saturation current, resistance, time and inductance respectively.

Given that the current becomes so percent of saturation current.
⇒0.8 Is=Is1-e-RtL

R=0.1Ω+0.9Ω=1Ω
⇒0.8=1-e-1×t10×10-3
⇒0.8=1-e-100t 
Therefore, the time is:
t=ln⁡11-0.8100=ln⁡5100=0.016 s

question:
An ideal coil of $10 \mathrm{H}$ is connected in series with a resistance of $5 \Omega$ and a battery of $5 \mathrm{~V}$. 
$2$ second after the connection is made the current flowing in amperes in the circuit is
solution:
$\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_0\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{Rt}}{\mathrm{L}}}\right)$
$=\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-1}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: Magnetic flux (in weber) in a closed circuit of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> varies with time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mfenced><mi>s</mi></mfenced></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The magnitude of the induced current at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mA</mi></math>.
question: Magnetic flux (in weber) in a closed circuit of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> varies with time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mfenced><mi>s</mi></mfenced></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The magnitude of the induced current at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mA</mi></math>.
solution:
Given that magnetic flux ϕ=8t2-9t+5

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction

emf=-dϕdt=-16t-9

At t=0.25 s

emf=-16×0.25-9=5 V

Current =emf  Resistance =5 V20 Ω

=14 A=10004 mA=250 mA

subject: Physics
q: A square loop of side $2$ cm is placed in a time varying magnetic field with magnitude as $B = 0.4\sin(300t)$ Tesla. The normal to the plane of loop makes an angle of $60°$ with the field. The maximum induced emf produced in the loop is __________ mV.
question: A square loop of side $2$ cm is placed in a time varying magnetic field with magnitude as $B = 0.4\sin(300t)$ Tesla. The normal to the plane of loop makes an angle of $60°$ with the field. The maximum induced emf produced in the loop is __________ mV.
solution:
Given:
Side of the square loop, $a = 2$ cm $= 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m
Area of the loop, $A = a^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m$^2$
Magnetic field, $B = 0.4 \sin(300t)$ T
Angle between the normal to the plane and the magnetic field, $\theta = 60^\circ$

The magnetic flux through the loop is given by:
$\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = B A \cos\theta$
$\Phi = (0.4 \sin(300t))(4 \times 10^{-4}) \cos(60^\circ)$
$\Phi = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \sin(300t) \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Phi = 0.8 \times 10^{-4} \sin(300t)$ Wb

By Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf is:
$e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$
$e = -\dfrac{d}{dt} (0.8 \times 10^{-4} \sin(300t))$
$e = -0.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 \cos(300t)$
$e = -240 \times 10^{-4} \cos(300t)$ V
$e = -24 \times 10^{-3} \cos(300t)$ V

The maximum induced emf is the amplitude of $e$:
$e_{\text{max}} = 24 \times 10^{-3}$ V $= 24$ mV

Answer: $24$

q: Consider a circular coil of wire carrying constant current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></math> forming a magnetic dipole. The magnetic flux through an infinite plane that contains the circular coil and excluding the circular coil area is given by <math><msub><mrow><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> The magnetic flux through the area of the circular coil area is given by <math><msub><mrow><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> . Which of the following option is correct?
question: Consider a circular coil of wire carrying constant current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></math> forming a magnetic dipole. The magnetic flux through an infinite plane that contains the circular coil and excluding the circular coil area is given by <math><msub><mrow><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> The magnetic flux through the area of the circular coil area is given by <math><msub><mrow><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> . Which of the following option is correct?
solution:
As magnetic field lines always form a closed loop, hence every magnetic field line creating magnetic flux in the inner region must be passing through the outer region. Since flux in two regions are in opposite direction.
∴ϕi=-ϕ0

subject: Physics
q: A circular current loop of radius $R$ is placed inside square loop of side length $L$ $(L >> R)$ such that they are co-planar and their centers coincide. The permeability of free space is $\mu_0$. The mutual inductance between circular loop and square loop is _______.
question: A circular current loop of radius $R$ is placed inside square loop of side length $L$ $(L >> R)$ such that they are co-planar and their centers coincide. The permeability of free space is $\mu_0$. The mutual inductance between circular loop and square loop is _______.
solution:
Using the reciprocity theorem, assume a current $I$ flows through the larger square loop and compute the flux through the smaller circular loop. The mutual inductance is given by $M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I}$.

Magnetic field at the centre of the square loop:
For a square of side $L$, the perpendicular distance from the centre to any side is $d = \dfrac{L}{2}$, and each side subtends angles $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 45^\circ$ at the centre.

The field due to one finite straight segment is:
$B_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi d}(\sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2)$

$B_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi \cdot \frac{L}{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi L} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{2\pi L}$

All four sides contribute equally in the same direction at the centre, so:
$B_{\text{total}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{2\pi L} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{\pi L}$

Flux through the circular loop:
Since $L \gg R$, the field $B_{\text{total}}$ can be treated as uniform over the area $A = \pi R^2$ of the circular loop.

$\Phi = B_{\text{total}} \cdot A = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{\pi L} \cdot \pi R^2 = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2 I}{L}$

Mutual inductance:
$M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2}{L}$

Hence, the correct option is $(4)\ \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2}{L}$.

subject: Physics
q:
Regarding self-inductance:
A. The self-inductance of the coil depends on its geometry.
B. Self-inductance does not depend on the permeability of the medium.
C. Self-induced e.m.f. opposes any change in the current in a circuit.
D. Self-inductance is electromagnetic analogue of mass in mechanics.
E. Work needs to be done against self-induced e.m.f. in establishing the current.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Regarding self-inductance:
A. The self-inductance of the coil depends on its geometry.
B. Self-inductance does not depend on the permeability of the medium.
C. Self-induced e.m.f. opposes any change in the current in a circuit.
D. Self-inductance is electromagnetic analogue of mass in mechanics.
E. Work needs to be done against self-induced e.m.f. in establishing the current.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
A. The self-inductance of the coil depends on its geometry.
This is true because the self-inductance of a coil is given by formulas that include its geometrical parameters (number of turns, cross-sectional area, length, etc.). For example, for a solenoid,
\(L=\mu_0 \mu_r \frac{N^2 A}{l}\)
where \(N\) is the number of turns, \(A\) is the cross-sectional area, and \(l\) is the length.
B. Self-inductance does not depend on the permeability of the medium.
This is false. The permeability of the medium (represented by \(\mu=\mu_0 \mu_r\)) directly influences the inductance, as seen in the formula above. So, any change in the medium's permeability will affect the inductance.
C. Self-induced e.m.f. opposes any change in the current in a circuit.
This is true, and it is a direct consequence of Lenz's law. The induced electromotive force (e.m.f.) always acts in a direction such that it opposes the change in current that produced it.
D. Self-inductance is the electromagnetic analogue of mass in mechanics.
This is true. Just as mass resists changes in velocity (inertia), inductance resists changes in current, which is why it is often compared to the inertial mass in mechanical systems.
E. Work needs to be done against self-induced e.m.f. in establishing the current.
This is true because, when you try to change the current, you must do work to overcome the opposing self-induced e.m.f., storing energy in the magnetic field of the inductor. Based on the above reasoning, the true statements are \(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}\), and E.
Thus, the correct answer is:
Option C
A, C, D, E only

subject: Physics
q: When a coil is placed in a time dependent magnetic field the power dissipated in it is $P$. The number of turns, area of the coil and radius of the coil wire are $N$, $A$ and $r$ respectively. For a second coils number of turns, area of the coil and radius of the coil wire are $2N$, $2A$ and $3r$ respectively. When the first coil is replaced with second coil the power dissipated in it is $\sqrt{2}\,\alpha P$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
question: When a coil is placed in a time dependent magnetic field the power dissipated in it is $P$. The number of turns, area of the coil and radius of the coil wire are $N$, $A$ and $r$ respectively. For a second coils number of turns, area of the coil and radius of the coil wire are $2N$, $2A$ and $3r$ respectively. When the first coil is replaced with second coil the power dissipated in it is $\sqrt{2}\,\alpha P$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
solution:
The induced emf in the coil is given by Faraday's law:

$E = N A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

The resistance of the coil is $R = \rho \dfrac{l}{a}$, where $l$ is the total length of the wire and $a$ is its cross-sectional area.

The length of the wire is $l = N (2\pi R_c)$, where $R_c$ is the radius of the coil. Since the area of the coil is $A = \pi R_c^2$, we have $R_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$.

Thus, $l = 2N\sqrt{\pi A}$.

The cross-sectional area of the wire is $a = \pi r^2$.

Therefore, the resistance is:

$R = \rho \dfrac{2N\sqrt{\pi A}}{\pi r^2} \propto \dfrac{N\sqrt{A}}{r^2}$

The power dissipated in the coil is:

$P = \dfrac{E^2}{R} \propto \dfrac{(N A)^2}{\dfrac{N\sqrt{A}}{r^2}} = N A^{3/2} r^2$

For the second coil, the parameters are $N' = 2N$, $A' = 2A$, and $r' = 3r$. The new power dissipated is:

$P' = P \left(\dfrac{2N}{N}\right) \left(\dfrac{2A}{A}\right)^{3/2} \left(\dfrac{3r}{r}\right)^2$

$P' = P (2) (2\sqrt{2}) (9) = 36\sqrt{2} P$

Given that $P' = \sqrt{2}\alpha P$, we can compare the expressions to find:

$\sqrt{2}\alpha = 36\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \alpha = 36$

Answer: $36$

q: Consider an electrical circuit containing a two way switch S. Initially S is open and then T1 is connected to T2. As the current in R=6 Ω attains a maximum value of steady-state level, T1 is disconnected from T2 and immediately connected to T3. Potential drop across r=3 Ω resistor immediately after T1 is connected to T3 is ______V. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
question: Consider an electrical circuit containing a two way switch S. Initially S is open and then T1 is connected to T2. As the current in R=6 Ω attains a maximum value of steady-state level, T1 is disconnected from T2 and immediately connected to T3. Potential drop across r=3 Ω resistor immediately after T1 is connected to T3 is ______V. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
solution:
When T1 and T2 are connected, then the steady state current in the inductor I=66=1 A

When T1 and T3 are connected then current through inductor remains same. So potential difference across 3 Ω

V=Ir=1×3=3 V

subject: Physics
q: Inductance of a coil with $10^{4}$ turns is 10 mH and it is connected to a dc source of 10 V with internal resistance of $10 \Omega$. The energy density in the inductor when the current reaches $\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)$ of its maximum value is $\alpha \pi \times \frac{1}{e^{2}} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} / \mathrm{A}$).
question: Inductance of a coil with $10^{4}$ turns is 10 mH and it is connected to a dc source of 10 V with internal resistance of $10 \Omega$. The energy density in the inductor when the current reaches $\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)$ of its maximum value is $\alpha \pi \times \frac{1}{e^{2}} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} / \mathrm{A}$).
solution:
$\mathrm{L}=10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}$
$\mathrm{N}=10^4$

$I_0=\frac{10}{10}=1 \mathrm{~A} \text { (max current) }$
$I=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}$
$E_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$
$B=\mu_0 n I$
$L=\mu_0 n^2 \pi R^2 \ell$
$E_d=\frac{\mu_0 n^2 I^2}{2}$
$=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^8 \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^2}}{2}$
$=\frac{20 \pi}{\mathrm{e}^2}$

question: A bar magnet is passing through a conducting loop of radius R with velocity v. The radius of the bar magnet is such that it just passes through the loop. The induced e.m.f. in the loop can be represented by the approximate curve:

 at this moment emf is maximum and negative

 Magnet is in middle of coil, at this moment emf is equal to zero

 Now again flux start changing however polarity of emf get reverse

 At this moment emf is again maximum

 emf again become zero

q: A circular coil of 1000 turns each with area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated about its vertical diameter at the rate of one revolution per second in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum voltage generation will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: A circular coil of 1000 turns each with area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated about its vertical diameter at the rate of one revolution per second in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum voltage generation will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Given, N=1000, A=1 m2 and B=0.07 T

The maximum emf generated is εmax=NBAω,

=NBA2πf

 =1000×0.07×1×2π1=140π

Thus, maximum emf is 439.6 V≈440 V.

subject: Physics
q: A coil of area A and N turns is rotating with angular velocity \(\omega\) in a uniform magnetic field \(\vec{B}\) about an axis perpendicular to \(\vec{B}\). Magnetic flux \(\varphi\) and induced \(\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon\) across it, at an instant when \(\vec{B}\) is parallel to the plane of coil, are :
question: A coil of area A and N turns is rotating with angular velocity \(\omega\) in a uniform magnetic field \(\vec{B}\) about an axis perpendicular to \(\vec{B}\). Magnetic flux \(\varphi\) and induced \(\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon\) across it, at an instant when \(\vec{B}\) is parallel to the plane of coil, are :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \phi & =\mathrm{BAN} \cdot \cos (\omega \mathrm{t}) \\ \varepsilon & =\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{BA} \omega \mathrm{~N} \cdot \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}) \end{aligned}$ When $B$ is parallel to plane, $\underline{\underline{\omega t}}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ $\Rightarrow \phi=0, \varepsilon=\mathrm{BA} \omega \mathrm{~N}$

subject: Physics
q: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
question: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=3 \mathrm{t}+8 \\ & \varepsilon=12 \mathrm{mV} \\ & |\varepsilon|=\mathrm{L}\left|\frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right| \\ & 12=\mathrm{L} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{~L}=4 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field B crossing normally a square metallic plate of area 4 m2 is changing with time as shown in figure. The magnitude of induced emf in the plate during t=2 s to t=4 s, is _____ mV.
question: The magnetic field B crossing normally a square metallic plate of area 4 m2 is changing with time as shown in figure. The magnitude of induced emf in the plate during t=2 s to t=4 s, is _____ mV.
solution:
From the graph, it can be seen that the value of the magnetic field at t=2 s is 4 mT, whereas the magnetic field at t=4 s is 8 mT.

The formula to calculate the magnitude of the induced emf is given by

εind =dϕdt=ddtBA=AdBdt   ...1

dBdt is the slope of the given graph.

Hence, the value of the induced emf can be calculated as follows:

εind =4 m2×8 mT-4 mT4 s-2 s=8 mV

subject: Physics
q: A circular loop of radius 7 cm is placed in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T directed perpendicular to plane of loop. The loop is converted into a square loop in 0.5 s. The EMF induced in the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mV.
question: A circular loop of radius 7 cm is placed in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T directed perpendicular to plane of loop. The loop is converted into a square loop in 0.5 s. The EMF induced in the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mV.
solution:
Circular loop with radius $r = 0.07$ m in magnetic field $B = 0.2$ T.
Initial flux through circular loop: $\Phi_1 = B \times \pi r^2 = 0.2 \times \pi(0.07)^2 = 0.000980\pi$ Wb
The loop is converted to a square maintaining the same perimeter: $C = 2\pi r = 0.14\pi$ m
Side of square: $a = \frac{0.14\pi}{4} = 0.035\pi$ m
Final flux through square loop: $\Phi_2 = B \times a^2 = 0.2 \times (0.035\pi)^2 = 0.2 \times 0.001225\pi^2$ Wb
Evaluating: $\Phi_1 = 0.2 \times 0.0154 = 0.00308$ Wb
$\Phi_2 = 0.2 \times 0.001225 \times 9.87 = 0.00242$ Wb
Change in flux: $\Delta\Phi = 0.00308 - 0.00242 = 0.00066$ Wb
Average EMF: $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.00066}{0.5} = 0.00132$ V $= 1.32$ mV

subject: Physics
q:
Consider \(\mathrm{I}_1\) and \(\mathrm{I}_2\) are the currents flowing simultaneously in two nearby coils 1 & 2, respectively. If \(\mathrm{L}_1=\) self inductance of coil \(1, \mathrm{M}_{12}=\) mutual inductance of coil 1 with respect to coil 2, then the value of induced emf in coil 1 will be
Options
question:
Consider \(\mathrm{I}_1\) and \(\mathrm{I}_2\) are the currents flowing simultaneously in two nearby coils 1 & 2, respectively. If \(\mathrm{L}_1=\) self inductance of coil \(1, \mathrm{M}_{12}=\) mutual inductance of coil 1 with respect to coil 2, then the value of induced emf in coil 1 will be
Options
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \phi_1=\mathrm{L}_1 \mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{M}_{12} \mathrm{I}_2 \\ & \varepsilon_1=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi_1}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{L}_1 \frac{\mathrm{dI}}{1} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{12}}{\mathrm{dt}}-\mathrm{M}_{12} \frac{\mathrm{~d} I_2}{\mathrm{dt}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A horizontal straight wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long extending from east to west falling freely at right angle to horizontal component of earth's magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>60</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The instantaneous value of emf induced in the wire when its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: A horizontal straight wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long extending from east to west falling freely at right angle to horizontal component of earth's magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>60</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The instantaneous value of emf induced in the wire when its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field, 

BH=0.60×10-4 Wb m-2.

Induced emf e=BHvℓ

=0.60×10-4×10×5

=3×10-3 V

subject: Physics
q:
For the given circuit the current through battery of 6 V just after closing the switch 'S' will be _____ A.

​​​​​​​
question:
For the given circuit the current through battery of 6 V just after closing the switch 'S' will be _____ A.

​​​​​​​
solution:
Just after closing the switch S, inductor behaves like an open circuit, so no current will flow through 2 Ω resistors.

Thus, current through the circuit is I=62+4=1 A (Here 2 Ω and 4 Ω resistors are in series).

subject: Physics
q: Two concentric circular coils with radii 1 cm and 1000 cm and number of turns 10 and 200 respectively are placed coaxially with centers coinciding. The mutual inductance of this arrangement will be _____ ×10–8 H. (Take, π2=10)
question: Two concentric circular coils with radii 1 cm and 1000 cm and number of turns 10 and 200 respectively are placed coaxially with centers coinciding. The mutual inductance of this arrangement will be _____ ×10–8 H. (Take, π2=10)
solution:
Let the magnetic field of the 200 turns be B1. The formula of magnetic field is given by B=μ0i2r

Let r=1000 cm. Thus, 

B1=nμ0×I2×10=200μ0I2×10

The flux through the coil of radius r1=1 cm is ϕ=10×B→·A→=10×B1πr21

⇒ϕ=10×200μ0I2×10×π0.012 

L=10×200μ02×10×π0.012=10×2004π×10-72×10×π0.012=4×10-8 H

q:
A conducting bar of length L is free to slide on two parallel conducting rails as shown in the figure

Two resistors R1 and R2 are connected across the ends of the rails. There is a uniform magnetic field B→ pointing into the page. An external agent pulls the bar to the left at a constant speed v.

The correct statement about the directions of induced currents I1 and I2 flowing through R1 and R2 respectively is :
question:
A conducting bar of length L is free to slide on two parallel conducting rails as shown in the figure

Two resistors R1 and R2 are connected across the ends of the rails. There is a uniform magnetic field B→ pointing into the page. An external agent pulls the bar to the left at a constant speed v.

The correct statement about the directions of induced currents I1 and I2 flowing through R1 and R2 respectively is :
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are in anticlockwise direction

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are in clockwise direction

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is in clockwise direction and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is in anticlockwise direction

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is in anticlockwise direction and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is in clockwise direction
solution:
Given, the external agent pulls the bar towards left. According to Lenz's law and Fleming's right hand rule, there will be a motional EMF generated in the bar. The right-hand rule tells us that the magnetic force on positive charges will be in downward direction and the electric equivalent circuit can be drawn as shown below.

So, I1 is in the clockwise direction and I2 is in the anticlockwise direction.

subject: Physics
q: Three identical coils $C_{1}, C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ are closely placed such that they share a common axis. $C_{2}$ is exactly midway. $C_{1}$ carries current $I$ in anti-clockwise direction while $C_{3}$ carries current $I$ in clockwise direction. An induced current flows through $C_{2}$ will be in clockwise direction when
question: Three identical coils $C_{1}, C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ are closely placed such that they share a common axis. $C_{2}$ is exactly midway. $C_{1}$ carries current $I$ in anti-clockwise direction while $C_{3}$ carries current $I$ in clockwise direction. An induced current flows through $C_{2}$ will be in clockwise direction when
options: $C_{1}$ moves away from $C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ moves towards $C_{2}$
$C_{1}$ and $C_{3}$ move with equal speeds away from $C_{2}$
$C_{1}$ moves towards $C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ moves away from $C_{2}$
$C_{1}$ and $C_{3}$ move with equal speeds towards $C_{2}$
solution:
Let the common axis be the z-axis.
$C_1$ is at $z = -d$, $C_2$ is at $z = 0$, and $C_3$ is at $z = +d$.
Current in $C_1$ is anti-clockwise (ACW), producing a magnetic field $B_1$ in the $+z$ direction at $C_2$.
Current in $C_3$ is clockwise (CW), producing a magnetic field $B_3$ in the $-z$ direction at $C_2$.
The net magnetic field at $C_2$ is $B_{net} = B_1 - B_3$. Since $C_1$ and $C_3$ are equidistant and carry equal current, initially $B_{net} = 0$.

To induce a clockwise current in $C_2$, Lenz's law states that the induced current must oppose the change in flux. A clockwise induced current produces a magnetic field in the $-z$ direction.
This happens if there is an increasing flux in the $+z$ direction or a decreasing flux in the $-z$ direction.

Case 1: $C_1$ moves towards $C_2$. The distance decreases, so $B_1$ (in $+z$ direction) increases. This causes an increasing flux in the $+z$ direction.

Case 2: $C_3$ moves away from $C_2$. The distance increases, so $B_3$ (in $-z$ direction) decreases. This causes a decreasing flux in the $-z$ direction.

Both actions (moving $C_1$ towards $C_2$ and moving $C_3$ away from $C_2$) result in a net change in flux that induces a clockwise current in $C_2$ to oppose the change.
Therefore, option (3) is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A square frame of side 10 cm and a long straight wire carrying current 1 A are in the plane of the paper. Starting from close to the wire, the frame moves towards the right with a constant speed of 10 m s-1 (see figure). The e.m.f induced at the time the left arm of the frame is at x=10 cm from the wire is
question: A square frame of side 10 cm and a long straight wire carrying current 1 A are in the plane of the paper. Starting from close to the wire, the frame moves towards the right with a constant speed of 10 m s-1 (see figure). The e.m.f induced at the time the left arm of the frame is at x=10 cm from the wire is
solution:
dϕ=μ0i2πyady

ϕ=μ0ia2π∫xx+adyy=μ0ia2πlnx+a-lnx

E.m.f=-dϕdt=-μ0ia2π1x+adxdt-1xdxdt

=μ0ia2πa.vxx+a=μ02π.ia2vxx+a=2×10-7×1×0.12×100.1×0.2=1μV

Alternative method:

Consider two sides which are perpendicular to the velocity as case of motional e.m.f.

E=E1-E2=B1–B2V l

=μ0I2π1x-1x+aV l

=2×10-7×110.1-10.2×10×0.1

=10-6 V=1 μV
